entry.title: Premium Hub CoE – DSC Knowledge Bits Series - SAP Signavio Process Intelligence for DSC
entry.content:
Solution Introduction

​SAP Signavio Process Intelligence is the process mining solution within SAP Signavio solution suite. With SAP Signavio Process Intelligence you can:

  • Perform process mining, analysis, simulation, modelling, and collaboration on the same collaborative environment.
  • Embed your insights and analyses from SAP Signavio Process Intelligence into your process models and journeys.
  • Connect your process models and journeys models with process mining and experience data.

Extract - Transfer - Load

First step to setup your SAP Signavio Process Intelligence solution is to create a data pipeline consisting of

  • one or multiple connected systems,
  • the source data consisting of select statements on relevant database tables (Extract),
  • the process data model including the case and event definition (Transfer),
  • and finally the analysis based on the previously defined data steps (Load)

The foundation in the setup of SAP Signavio Process Intelligence is the knowledge about the data model of the to-be analysed solution, to enable efficient and on-the-point case and event definition.

Process Mining - Investigations

Once the setup is completed and refined, the process mining can be performed. With SAP Signavio Process Intelligence you can

  • Accelerate understanding of correlations between process performance and influencing factors
  • Generate BPMN models based on extracted data
  • Analyze process variants to understand which events are actually posted in which sequence
  • Check for process conformance by comparing actually "mined" events against created BPMNs

Process Mining - Dashboards

Create dashboards based on defined process model (case/events) to drill-down into your productive data to compare e.g. warehouse performance in terms of cycle-times between events, usage of exception codes, completion rates, and more.

The dashboards can be configured based on configuration or low-code (SQL) avoiding complex and time-consuming implementation efforts.

To accelerate SAP Signavio Process Intelligence implementation, there are to Value Accelerators for Extended Warehouse Management available including Data Model, Investigation and Dashboard contents:

  • Improve On-Time Delivery Performance for Managing Warehouse and Inventory (outbound) in SAP Extended Warehouse Management

  • Reduce Revenue Loss Due to Stock-outs for Managing Warehouse and Inventory (outbound) in SAP Extended Warehouse Management

From Premium Engagement Services, we believe that you can achieve most efficiently results once you are thinking about process mining from the result - KPIs and analysis patterns to start with and model Cases, Events and Process Investigations and Dashboards based on that.
 
Learn more about SAP Premium Engagement and how a global team of experts can help you to get the most out of your SAP solutions.

If you already are a Premium Engagement customer, contact your Technical Quality Manager (TQM) to discuss how Premium Engagement services can help you with a custom-tailored and efficient service portfolio to make your process mining initiative a success!

#CoE EMEA SCM1

entry.title: Layout-Oriented Storage control (LOSC) In simple Inbound process
entry.content:
LOSC is typically used in warehouses where the product cannot be moved to destination bin directly due to the physical layout restrictions of the warehouse. For example, products stored in the mezzanine floor are received in the ground floor. In order to store the products in the mezzanine floor, they have to be transferred via elevator point. In such scenarios, LOSC is the better option, as it creates tasks to move products from one location to anther and provides product visibility in each of these areas.

 Figure 1

 

  1. Configuration

     1.1 Define Storage Types

SPRO –> IMG—> SCM Extended warehouse management –> Extended warehouse management –> Master Data –> Define Storage types.

Check Intermediate Storage type: S071 and Storage type role: A-Identification point

 Figure 2

 

Destination Storage type L0SC(standard)

 Figure 3

 

 1.2 Define Layout-Oriented Storage process control (LOSC)

SPRO –> IMG—> SCM Extended warehouse management –> Extended warehouse management –> Cross-process settings –> Warehouse Task –> Define Layout-Oriented Storage process control

 Figure 4

 

1.3 Define control indicator for determining Warehouse Process Type (WPT)

SPRO –> IMG—> SCM Extended warehouse management –> Extended warehouse management –> Cross-process settings –> Warehouse Task –> Define Warehouse Process Type.

Check WPT 3030(WPT category 3-internal WH movement) this combination helps in Moving stock from 9010 to S071

 Figure 5

 

Process Steps

1.Create Material Master

Create material by selecting required views.

In /n/scwm/MAT1, maintain putaway control indicator as shown in figure 6

 Figure 6

 

2.Create purchase order

Create a purchase order in ERP system (T code ME21N), verify the vendor, plant and storage location for the entered product. The confirmation key for the PO line item should allow the creation of inbound deliveries.

 

3.Create an Inbound delivery

Create an inbound delivery in ERP system (T code VL31N),in the delivery detail screen, enter the delivery quantity.in the packing screen, pack the product into HU, since HU is a requirement for both POSC & LOSC.  Once the required data is entered save the data in the inbound delivery, this will create an inbound delivery in the ERP system and will be transferred to EWM system for further processing.

 Figure 7

 

4.Crate bins for L0SC storage type

 Crate bins in L0SC storage type by using T code /N/SCWM/LS01

 Figure 8

 

5.Maintain Inbound delivery

Maintain Inbound delivery in EWM system using T code /N/SCWM/PRDI, paste the inbound delivery created in the ERP system and execute it, do goods receipt and create warehouses task as shown in figure 9.

 Figure 9

 

Display the Inbound delivery in the warehouse monitor (T code /N/SCWM/MON), Here 2 warehouse tasks will be created, one for moving the HU from 9010 to S071 and another for moving the HU from S071 to L0SC storage type.

 Figure 10

 

Confirm the warehouse task in background

 Figure 11

 

After confirming the warehouse task in background, the source storage type for the second warehouse task changes from 9010 to S071 shown in figure 12.

 Figure 12

 

Now confirm the other open warehouse task in background as shown in figure 13

 Figure 13

 

Finally check the stock in the monitor in (T code/N/SCWM/MON) HU is moved to destination storage type L0SC and bin as shown in figure 14

 Figure 14

 

Conclusion:

From the above steps, one can understand how to configure Layout-Oriented Storage control (LOSC) In Inbound process, by following the above steps one can have a clear idea about when & how to use LOSC concept and can implement the same depends on the client business requirement.

 

References:

https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blogs-by-members/layout-oriented-storage-control-process-in-ewm/ba-p/13203240

www.ewmguru.com

www.sap-press.com

 

Pre requisite: Should have Basic knowledge of SAP Extended Warehouse Management.

 

Please like, share and comment for any queries through add comment button which is displayed at bottom of the blog-post.

For any queries, please post your question by using the following link

https://community.sap.com/t5/c-khhcw49343/SAP+Extended+Warehouse+Management/pd-p/01200615320800000705

Hope this blog-post will be help full

 

Thank you

entry.title: Concept for Warehouse Billing in EWM
entry.content:
Hi All

I am going to explain warehouse billing Concept in EWM.

Introduction- In EWM, we can record the quantity of warehouse services used for the services agreed upon in an agreement in the Transportation Management. We can subsequently send this quantity information back to the TM for charge calculation and settlement based on the charges you have agreed to in the agreement

Benefits-

1- Sell warehouse services to customers and bill the customers periodically based the services used for a time period

2- Purchase warehouse services from external service providers and periodically pay the service provider based on the warehouse services used for a time period.

Available Features in Warehouse Billing

1- Snapshot management
2- Warehouse billing measurement request management
3- Warehouse billing measurement management

We will describe each of one 

1- Snapshot Management - We use this feature to capture the EWM business data relevant to measuring warehouse services and for calculating the charges for billing

We can record below mentioned business data in snapshot management

1- Snapshot of Physical Stock - We create a snapshot for stock at a particular time. The system records the warehouse inventory data at this particular time. We can create snapshots to record the presence of physical stock on a daily basis. If We create multiple snapshots in one day, the system merges the physical stock on that specific date.

2- Snapshot for Inbound Deliveries - The system records business data for goods that have been received and confirmed in Inbound Delivery Document (Document Type PDI). If the inbound delivery items are partially received, the item is recorded partially with the quantity

3- Snapshot for Outbound Deliveries-The system records business data for goods that have been sent and have a completed status in Outbound Delivery Document (Document Type FDO).

4- Snapshot for Warehouse tasks-The system records data for warehouse tasks that have been carried out and confirmed

We below Scenarios to perform 

 1- We can Create Snapshots to create snapshots on a daily basis
2- Configure Snapshots to activate/deactivate, and specify other settings for the snapshots
3- To create snapshots using specify attributes, we can create snapshots for Physical stock, Inbound               deliveries, Outbound deliveries and warehouse tasks.

We can delete and remove snapshots

2- Request for Warehouse Billing Measurement- EWM automatically creates a request for warehouse billing measurement (WBMR) from the Transportation Management (TM) agreement data. In the WBMR, we can assign warehouse billing measurement services to measure the warehouse services used, and periodically create warehouse billing measurements (WBMs) for calculating the quantity of services used for a billing period. The system uses the WBM information for calculating charges for the warehouse services used in the TM. When agreement information in TM is changed, the WBMR reflects the changes in the EWM.

WBMR Status

In Process
Activated
Deactivated

When you save and release an agreement in TM, EWM automatically receives the agreement as a request for warehouse billing measurement (WBMR). The WBMR has the same billing schedule determined in the agreement in TM. The billing schedule refers to a specified factory calendar both in TM and EWM. You must specify the same factory calendar settings in both the systems.

Warehouse Billing Measurement Service Assignment 

We can calculate the quantity of warehouse services used after you assign a warehouse billing measurement service to an item in a request for warehouse billing measurement document. We can measure the service quantity for the following EWM documents
Number of documents

Product quantity

Weight

Volume

Number of bins

Number of handling units

We can also specify a variant along with the warehouse billing measurement service in the WBMR item to enter specific constraints to measure warehouse services. For example, we can constrain the measurement to a specific group of documents, products, bins, or handling unit.

Note-The system subsequently measures the quantity from EWM business data in snapshots

3- Warehouse Billing Measurement Management-

After we have activated a request for billing measurement, we can display the generated warehouse billing measurements (WBM) in the Warehouse Management Monitor under Billing Warehouse Billing Measurement. The WBM contains the quantity of warehouse services used. A WBM can be generated for every billing period we have defined. The quantity in the WBM is obtained from the business data recorded into the snapshots. In the Warehouse Management Monitor, you can see the business data that was used to calculate the quantity as the proof of services.

Billing measurement document has following statuses

Ready for Distribution
Distributed
No Distribution

Reporting for Warehouse Billing in EWM Warehouse Monitor

For reporing we can use warehouse management monitor-Billing-Snapshots
For reporting we can use warehouse management monitor - Billing - Request for Warehouse Billing Measurement.

For reporing we can use warehouse management monitor-Billing-Billing Measurement

 

Please share your feedback to improve it 

 

Reference-https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/9832125c23154a179bfa1784cdc9577a/5ad63855b04c6a38e10000000a174cb4.html

 

 

Regerads

Rashid

entry.title: EWM /SCWM/ISU - INITIAL STOCK UPLOAD AND REVERSEL WITH SERIAL NUMBER
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog I'll try to explain how to upload and reverse initial stock for materials with serial numbers both warehouse number level and stock level.

Steps For Excel Template
First you need to select post type according the data you enter the file.
If you enter material data you should choose 4 as process type. And for serial number it will be 6 and so on.



For warehouse and stock level excel template should be prepared as followed;
Note: For different material attributes may require extra fields entered.

Sample Templates

Item For HU

Item For Material

Items For Serial Numbers


Note: You can choose separator as below for CSV format. If the field separator is empty CSV format will apply
";" as default value.


Reversal
/SCWM/ADGI screen can be used for reverse the stock upload. Cancel Stock Migration and Cancel GI could be choosed but this options differs according to posting date. If you want to enter different posting date you should choose Cancel Stock Migration. Cancel GI is using the first material document posting date. After selecting GI Process Type,warehouse tasks number that related to material document need to entered to"GI documents field". 



In same cases /SCWM/ADGI is not working for reversal of initial stock material document. If stock is uploaded warehouse number level serialized material with HU. You'll get "/SCWM/GM45" error while try to reverse it at /SCWM/ADGI screen. In that case MIGO screen can be used.



And also /scwm/adgı reverse the whole material document, not allow to reverse partially. For partial reversal MIGO can be used with Material Item Number

 Example of a partial reversal of a material document with 2 Items;



Sample Reversal

Go to MIGO, Material document should be entered as followed;


Select the item that you want to reversed



Movement type should be changed as 562



Serial number must be entered for selected item



Check the storage bin information. It should be the same with the one you entered for stock upload. And you can post it.








I made all the test at 2022 version S4/HANA.

CSV Sample File Note 974852 : https://me.sap.com/notes/974852
Thanks
Ömer Burak Gedik

entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 01 - History of SAP Extended Warehouse Management
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog post for the SAP EWM Podcast Episode 01 – The history of SAP EWM.

You can listen to the podcast episode in English and in German on Spotify, Amazon Music, Apple Podcasts or Sway! Enjoy!

A look back to the 90s!

Warehouse management is not a new topic at SAP. Already in 1991 SAP released LES-WM as part of SAP R/3. At the beginning of 2002 SAP decided to develop a new warehouse management product - SAP Extended Warehouse Management. There were several reasons for the new development for example increased performance requirements, a more flexible process design, the direct integration of material flow systems or mobile working capabilities.

In 2005 SAP published the first release of SAP EWM. And since 2005 SAP delivers continously new functions and innovations for EWM to its customers. That means SAP develops warehouse management solutions for more than 30 Years now. 

The technical foundation of SAP EWM has changed several times in the past. At the beginning it was implemented on the Supply Chain Management platform, later – with EWM 9.0 - it was also possible to run it as a standalone solution on the Netweaver stack. Usually always as a decentralized system.

Warehouse Management with SAP - More than 30 years of experience

 

The move to SAP S/4HANA

In 2015  we launched SAP S/4HANA – our new ERP flagship and the successor of SAP ECC 6.0

And since 2016 EWM is available on SAP S/4HANA. With SAP S/4HANA release 1610 and 1709 SAP EWM was only available as an integrated warehouse management solution - the so-called embedded EWM in S / 4HANA. Because it was very clear that a state-of-the-art warehouse management solution should be integral part of SAP S/4HANA. With the release 1809 in the year 2018, the decentralized deployment option for EWM on S/4HANA was launched too.

Today customers have the option to use EWM either within an S/4HANA system or still as a decentralized system - either connected to an SAP ECC or a SAP S/4HANA system via native SAP to SAP integration. The decision whether embedded or decentralized is the better choice for a customer depends on various aspects - functional, operational and strategic aspects. Our podcast episode 2 is about this decision. 

Options for inventory management and warehouse management with SAPBasic vs. advanced Warehousing

 

For embedded EWM in S/4HANA there are also two operating models: Basic and advanced warehousing. Basic warehousing describes the license-free option comparable with LE-WM in SAP ECC. It's from a commercial perspective part of SAP S/4HANA and offers already features such as multi-level processes, handling unit management or the complete mobile working environment.

Advanced warehousing allows customers to use the full range of EWM functions to run and optimize their warehouses. It offers for example an integrated material flow control, a highly flexible wave management, kitting and value added services, work in process handling and many more. Advanced warehousing is a "best of breed" warehouse management solution combined with SAP's "best of suite" offering. 

The operating model basic warehousing is only available with embedded EWM in S/4HANA. And customers can choose on warehouse number level if a warehouse runs with basic or advanced warehousing. A decentralized EWM on S/4HANA however is always "advanced warehousing". From a source code perspective, there is no difference between basic and advanced warehousing. The complete source code is always available.

High Level Overview Basic vs. advanced Warehousing

 

The future of warehouse management with SAP

SAP EWM  on S/4HANA is the successor of SAP LE-WM. The mainstream maintenance for LE-WM as part of SAP ECC will end at the latest by end of 2027. There are different options to move forward with existing LE-WM Implementations. 

As part of an SAP S/4HANA migration customers can continue to use LE-WM in SAP S/4HANA as so called compatibility scope until end of 2025 for example to migrate other modules first or not migrate all warehouses to EWM at once.

There is also option of Stock Room Management which is part of SAP S/4HANA and which can be used in S/4HANA also after 2025. From a source code perspective it is LE-WM however some functionalities such as the decentralized WM or the material flow connection (LE-WCU Interface) are no longer part of Stock Room Management. Stock Room Management enables customers to run LE-WM implementations for simple warehouses without any adjustments also in S/4HANA. There is a report which supports customers to check if existing LE-WM implementations are Stock Room Management compliant that means that the implementations do not use any features which are not covered by the usage rights of Stock Room Management. It also important to mention that no further developments are planned for Stock Room Management. 

Stock Room Management as a bridging technology on the way to SAP EWM

 

And therefore it makes sense to discuss the migration of all LE-WM implementations to EWM. For new warehouse implementations SAP EWM is recommended. With embedded EWM SAP offers an integrated warehouse management solution in SAP S/4HANA that meets the latest logistics requirements. It is seamlessly integrated in S/4HANA. A very good example are the synchronous postings of goods receipt for production directly onto SAP EWM storage bins with the well-know transaction MIGO. In addition, users benefit from the intuitive SAP FIORI strategy and warehouses can be managed based on real-time KPIs defined with capabilities of embedded analytics.

SAP EWM - A holistic and integrated intralogistics platform

 

SAP EWM offers customers the best of both worlds - a best of breed WMS which is natively integrated in to SAP's solution portfolio in a best of suite approach. 

Helpful Links: 

SAP S/4HANA Feature Scope Description (basic vs. advanced warehousing)
SAP Note 2881166 - FAQ Stock Room Management
SAP Note 2577428 - Roadmap for LE-WM in S/4HANA

Please visit our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page with all Episodes, Blog Posts and Podcast Notes!

entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 02 - Integrated or Decentralized Warehouse Management with SAP EWM
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog post for the SAP EWM Podcast Episode 02 – Integrated or Decentralized Warehouse Management with SAP EWM - What Should I Do?

You can listen to the podcast episode in English and in German on Spotify, Amazon Music, Apple Podcasts or Sway! Enjoy!

Embedded vs. decentralized EWM 

Embedded EWM is part of SAP S/4HANA and can only be used with an existing S/4 installation. It is the EWM that is fully integrated into SAP S/4HANA  and ist is used in the same system like other logistic applications MM, SD, LE etc.. There are benefits of an embedded EWM for example here is no master data distribution from the ERP module and embedded EWM supports synchronous postings. 

Decentralized EWM means a separate installation that is supplied with the necessary data via native SAP to SAP interfaces, but on the other hand can work completely autonomously.

SAP EWM Deployment Options

 

Decision Criteria 

There are functional, operational, and strategic aspects that should be considered when you have to take a decision. 

Decision criteria embedded vs. decentral EWM

1) Functional decision criteria

The first thing to consider when making this decision is the functional differences between the two options: To make an example: If you want to connect several ERP clients or ERP systems to an EWM, you cannot use an embedded EWM for this but only a decentralized EWM. Beyond that capability there are rather soft functional criteria such as synchronous production integration in embedded EWM, which of course provides real added value especially for production-related warehouses. Soft criteria however means also that the processes are  supported in embedded EWM as well as in decentralized EWM. Often the differences are rather technical - from an end-user perspective there is normally no differences. 

The functional differences are described for each release in the corresponding release notes. There is also an PDF attachment to the notes which shows the differences in transparent table format: 

Excerpt from the functional comparison for SAP EWM on S/4HANA 2023

 

SAP Note 3376455 - Release information and restrictions of Decentralized EWM on SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS00, FPS01 and FPS02
SAP Note 3376453 - SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS00, FPS01 and FPS02: Release information and restrictions for EWM in SAP S/4HANA

2) Operational decision criteria

Operational decision criteria assess the influence of a deployment on the operation of the solution. The independence of warehouse management from the ERP system for example is one of the major operational aspects. Independence can mean different things: For example the fact that if there is a downtime in the ERP, the warehouse can continue to work for the time being. Purchase orders that have already been distributed can be received and distributed outbound deliveries can be picked and shipped. The standard integration of both systems ensures that everything is posted correctly after the connection is restored.

This is ensured by the native SAP to SAP integration between EWM and ERP which uses qRFCs (queued remote function calls) for the exchange of transactional data which offer an error-tolerant integration close to real time. This native integration supports for example the distribution of purchase orders or late changes of customer orders and outbound deliveries on ERP side with automatic updates on EWM side.  

Another operational aspect is the performance and latency of the EWM systems. It can make sense or it can be necessary to operate EWM system close to the  warehouses. This does not mean that the systems have to be at the warehouse location, this is in general not necessary. But it could be required that the data center of a hypersaler is at least in the same region than the warehouse for example in Europe, Americas or in Asia. In this case a decentralized EWM is necessary if there is a single ERP system hosted for example in Europe. 

A decentralized EWM also gives more independence when it comes to maintenance and release cycles. Embedded EWM implementations must be upgraded together with the SAP S/4HANA ERP system. This can lead to bottlenecks in project and test capacities or you simply don't want to take the risk of a joint upgrade for all divisions and all warehouses.

Technical dependency can also play a role. In a decentralized EWM landscape customers do not have to coordinate with the entire SAP S/4HANA system when importing process changes. This can definitely increase the flexibility of logistics and IT departments. And finally customers have to check whether their SAP S/4HANA system has sufficient resources to operate the entire warehouse management - it's about HANA memory, application servers and so on. 

There are technical capabilities for 'embedded' EWM to get the technical infrastructure level more similar to the 'decentralized' EWM in which a whole ABAP system is dedicated for EWM for example by the placement of the EWM table group to a dedicated HANA DB scale-out node or by using dedicated application servers only for EWM users. Please check the following SAP notes for more details: 

1423066 - Optimization of the performance in EWM
2950948 - EWM table group for placement on HANA DB scale-out node

To clarify system performance aspect a greenfield system sizing should also be made as part of the deployment decision. Especially for high-volume and high performance warehouses with a lot of warehouse automation the sizing is a decisive point. The topic of automation is also always an important point: With EWM the whole material flow can be control with the integrated material flow system (EWM-MFS). External material flow systems are no longer required and EWM communicates directly with the PLCs.

Warehouse automation is a strong argument for the decentralized approach - but not one that excludes the embedded EWM option. You really have to check the specific requirements. There are SAP EWM customers who control their automated warehouses directly with embedded EWM. This can also work without any problems. A sizing you is highly recommended when automation is used. 

Sizing for SAP Extended Warehouse Management

 Please check the following links to get more information about sizing for EWM: 

SAP Sizing - HANA based Quicksizer 
SAP Note 1896197 - Sizing of Extended Warehouse Management - Best Practices

2) Strategic decision criteria

The central question when it comes to strategic decision criteria is “how will my business and my logistics develop in the future?". Answers to questions like "Do we expect strong growth", "do we have an automation strategy" or "will we have many new logistics sites" influence the decision and can rather speak for a decentralized approach - to ensure independence and performance. 

It is also conceivable to start the S/4HANA journey with a decentralized EWM on S/4HANA: We call this "logistics first approach". Customers can thus reduce the complexity of a transformation, as they only have to deal with warehouse logistics in the first step.  Later the other SAP modules follow. The integration of the decentralized EWM on S/4HANA is then only be switched from ECC to S/4HANA. Thanks to the same technical integration the complexity of switching is low and the effort is manageable.

Logistics first approach with SAP EWM

Hybrid approaches are also possible and on the roadmap of many SAP customers. This means that embedded EWM is for example used for smaller sales warehouses or production-related warehouses and decentralized EWM is used for the large distribution centers. The big advantage is that customers can rely on one single solution for their whole intralogistics - in production and distribution - since the source code and the application is the same. Even re-use of customer specific EWM extensions is in a hybrid landscape possible. 

Summary  

The answer to the question "embedded or decentralized EWM - what should I do?" depends on many aspects. In addition it is not simply possible to switch between the deployment options just by configuration. It is possible due to the fact that there is only one EWM allowing both options but the switch is always a migration project. In order to come to a good decision, it is required that customers do an individual assessment of the criteria mentioned above. Assessment tools like a utility analysis, decision trees other methods can support this process.

Helpful Links: 

SAP Note 1606493 - SAP EWM Deployment Options Best Practices

Please visit our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page with all Episodes, Blog Posts and Podcast Notes!

entry.title: An Overview for Warehouse Order in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Hi All 

In this blog I will discuss an overview for warehouse Order

Introduction- 

Document that represents an executable work package that a warehouse employee should perform at a specific time. The warehouse order consists of warehouse tasks or physical inventory items. When we move products, EWM create warehouse tasks, then EWM groups these warehouse tasks together into warehouse orders, and makes them available for processing. When we perform a physical inventory, we create physical inventory items, EWM also groups these physical inventory items together into warehouse orders. We can also use warehouse order together with wave management. We can also use warehouse orders together with wave management. Waves are used to group warehouse requests to allow the creation of multiple warehouse tasks at the same time. We can schedule the release of waves for task creation to enable efficient task management in the warehouse. These tasks can then be grouped into warehouse orders for further assignment to resources.

Configuring steps for Warehouse Orders

The configuration settings required for filters, limits, packing profiles, and the like. These settings help set up an executable work package for the warehouse resource. The resource can complete multiple open tasks in an activity area together, thus reducing travel into and out of the warehouse for each task execution.

Define Sort rules for warehouse task

Use this setting to define the sort rules and assign them to one or more sort fields. Click on New Entries, and, for each sort rule, define the fields in the warehouse tasks that can be used for sorting and whether they need to be sorted in ascending or descending order.

IMG  • Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Warehouse Order • Define Sort Rules for Warehouse Tasks

Filters for warehouse order creation rule

Filters are used to set rules for processing a warehouse task. Embedded EWM  Warehouse Order applies filters on warehouse tasks that have not been assigned to any warehouse order to check if they can be processed by a warehouse order creation rule.

IMG-Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Warehouse Order • Define Filters for Warehouse Order Creation Rules

There are two types of filters that can be used in warehouse order creation based on the value set in Filter Type

Item filter

Item filters are used to check individual warehouse tasks for a warehouse order and are defined by setting Filter Type to I Filter at Item Level. The system checks to see if the warehouse task is within the min/max volume, weight, and processing limits of the item filter. It also checks whether the warehouse task has been created using a specific warehouse process type, route, wave category, and reason for movement. If a warehouse task doesn’t meet the filtering criteria, system checks to see if it can use the next warehouse order creation rule in the sequence to process the warehouse task.

Subtotal filter

Embedded EWM uses subtotal filters to compare several warehouse tasks against a subtotal; they’re defined by setting Filter Type to S Filter at Subtotal Level. To use subtotal filters, it’s important to sort warehouse tasks using sorting rules with a sort field consolidation group.

Limit value for size of warehouse order

The limit value of a warehouse order creation rule specifies the size of a warehouse order. The limit value is assigned to each warehouse order creation rule. Warehouse tasks assigned to a warehouse order creation rule are assessed for limit values, which are used to set a limit on the size of the warehouse order. As soon as the warehouse task exceeds any of the limit filter criteria, it’s taken out of the existing warehouse order and assigned to a new warehouse order using the same warehouse order creation rule. The system again applies a limit filter to the warehouse tasks assigned to the new warehouse order. A limit filter can be used to set limits for maximum/ minimum warehouse tasks in a warehouse order, maximum/minimum weight, volume, processing time, and so on

IMG-Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Warehouse Order • Define Limit Values for the Size of a Warehouse Order

Note - You can set a limit value type (Limit Val. Type) of Physical Inventory if warehouse tasks included in the warehouse order are for physical inventory

Packing profile

 Packing profiles are used to create pick HUs during the creation of warehouse orders for outbound/picking processes. Using a packing profile, the system determines a packaging specification and HU type using the packaging material master data for the pick HU. The system uses data such as weight, volume, capacity, and so on from the assigned warehouse order when determining the appropriate number of pick HUs in the embedded EWM system. For example, if the assigned warehouse tasks don’t fit into one HU, then the system creates another pick HU using a predefined packaging material

IMG -Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Warehouse Order • Define Packing Profile for Warehouse Order Creation

Note- There are multiple parameters that control the creation of pick Hus

Pack Mode

Sorting

Create HU

Assign Warehouse Task to HU

Split Warehouse Task

Split Warehouse Task Based on AUoM

SKIP Warehouse TasK

Check LWH

Warehouse order creation rule

The filters, profiles, and limits described previously are used to create a container in the form of a warehouse order creation rule that creates warehouse orders for open warehouse tasks. The warehouse  order creation rule also contains the time required for the worker to complete the warehouse order

IMG- Extended Warehouse Management • Cross Process Settings • Warehouse Order • Define Creation Rules for Warehouse Order

You can review the created warehouse order creation rules.

IMG-Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Warehouse Order • Overview of Creation Rules for Warehouse Orders

Determination of warehouse order creation rule

Warehouse orders are used to group multiple warehouse tasks in a single work package. To determine which warehouse order creation rule is applicable for a warehouse task, a search sequence for the warehouse order creation rule can be assigned to a combination of activity area and activity.

IMG-• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Warehouse Order • Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas

Note - You can define more than one warehouse order creation rule for an activity area and activity. This way, the system searches for a warehouse order creation rule based on the defined search sequence and picks the first warehouse order creation rule that fits the required filtering criteria. For warehouse order creation rule determination, it’s mandatory to sort the storage bins for activity area and activity.

Manual Assembly of Warehouse Orders

This function is used in the goods receipt process to delete warehouse tasks of different HUs from an existing warehouse order and group them in a new warehouse order using both SAP GUI and RF. This function is optimized so that you can operate it using a hand-held scanner that is installed on a keyboard filter. You can specify multiple HUs in the entry field, one after each other. EWM shows you the specified HU in the lower part of the screen, and assigns the HU to a position field, ascending. For example, you scan three HUs. EWM assigns the HU scanned first to position one, the next HU to position two, and the HU scanned last to position three. Make sure that the position of the HU in EWM matches the physical position of the HU in the resource. When you perform putaway, EWM shows you the position in the Radio Frequency dialog .

Green- you have specified a valid HU, and that warehouse tasks exist for all products in the HU. EWM has updated screen content for the total weight and total volume, and increased the number of HUs in the new warehouse order that is to be created by one.

Red-you have specified an HU, but that no warehouse task exists for at least one of the products in the HU. EWM does not update the screen content for the total weight and total volume, and does not include the HU in the new warehouse order that is to be created.

Printing Warehouse Orders

EWM allows automatic printing of warehouse orders during their creation using smart forms or SAPscript. The warehouse order printing activity also can be carried out from the warehouse monitor. The printing is carried out via the PPF, along with the use of a condition technique. For customizing go in below path.

IMG-Extended Warehouse Management • Warehouse Order • Print • Assign Determination Procedure.

  • EWM automatically triggers the print run from processing, or you trigger it manually
  • EWM determines the print determination procedure assigned to the warehouse process type
  • PPF calls the /SCWM/WME application with action profile /SCWM/WO and checks for the condition record for the print action definition
  • On successfully finding both the schedule and trigger condition, the system triggers the function module to print the SAP Smart Form.
  • The PPF saves the action executed and issues print logs

Some Action Definitions and Smart Forms

Note- If there are no more warehouse order creation rules left in the sequence for a warehouse order, the system assigns them to default rule UNDE.

 

Thanks
